text.title: Fast vs Slow Burn Rule Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the Fast vs Slow Burn Rule methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every SLO MUST have 4 alert windows: 1h fast (14.4x burn), 6h sustained (6x burn), 3d slow (1x burn), 30d trend (0.1x burn).
rule.rationale: Single-window alerts produce false positives or false negatives; 4 windows triangulate.

rule.statement: 1h + 6h windows MUST page on-call. 3d window MUST create a ticket (no page). 30d window MUST notify the review channel (no page, no ticket).
rule.rationale: Page cadence matches action cadence: 1h needs now-action; 30d needs awareness.

rule.statement: Burn-rate thresholds MUST be calibrated to SLO objective: budget_consumed_in_window / budget_for_window. Hardcoded thresholds (e.g. error_rate > 0.001) forbidden.
rule.rationale: Hardcoded thresholds don't track SLO; burn-rate-based thresholds keep alerting consistent across SLOs.

rule.statement: Burn-rate PromQL MUST reference a recording-rule series (level:metric:rate1h, rate6h, rate3d, rate30d). Ad-hoc queries forbidden.
rule.rationale: Ad-hoc queries drift; recording rules ensure all alerts use the same SLI.

rule.statement: Every alert (4 per SLO) MUST carry runbook_url annotation pointing to a written runbook step. Runbooks differ by window (1h: investigate now; 30d: review trend).
rule.rationale: Without runbook, on-call pages translate to scrambling; runbook URL is the entry point.

rule.statement: Burn-rate thresholds recalibrated quarterly when SLO target changes. New targets MUST recompute all 4 windows + redeploy alerts.
rule.rationale: SLO change without alert recalibration leaves a gap between alert and policy.
